Summary: This study compares the performance of conventional radiomics and deep learning (CNN) algorithms in predicting overall survival (OS) in HCC patients using CT data. The study found that CNN models generally outperformed conventional radiomics models, but the clinical Cox-regression model, incorporating clinical parameters, achieved the best validation performance for OS prediction.

Item1.reason: The study does not explicitly mention adherence to specific radiomics or machine learning checklists or guidelines like the CLEAR checklist.

Item2.reason: The study defines inclusion and exclusion criteria for patient selection, aiming to create a representative study population. Inclusion criteria are related to HCC diagnosis confirmation by imaging or histopathology, availability of contrast-enhanced CT scans before therapy, and initial therapy carried out at the hospital. Exclusion criteria include incomplete CT scans, severe artifacts, and other active tumor diseases.

Item3.reason: The reference standard is overall survival (OS), which is a well-established clinical outcome. The OS was calculated from the initial CT scan to the time of death or last contact. The diagnosis of HCC was confirmed by either histopathology or a second imaging modality, according to the German HCC guideline.

Item4.reason: The CT scans were acquired on 24 different scanners at 21 medical centers.

Item5.reason: The study uses contrast-enhanced CT scans of the liver, which is a standard imaging technique for HCC diagnosis and staging. The imaging data source is clinically translatable.

Item6.reason: The study mentions common contrast media methods and refers to Supplementary Table S1 for the variability of scan parameters. This provides some information on the imaging protocol, although a more detailed description would be ideal.

Item7.reason: Overall survival was calculated as the period from the initial CT scan to the time of either death or last contact to our hospital. This is appropriate to validate the presence or absence of target conditions of the radiomics analysis at the moment of the diagnostic imaging exams.

Condition1.reason: The study includes segmentation of the liver parenchyma and tumor findings on CT images.

Condition2.reason: The study mentions that a resident contoured the liver parenchyma and the largest HCC-lesion, indicating manual segmentation.

Item8.reason: The study mentions that a resident with two years of experience in liver imaging contoured the liver parenchyma and the largest HCC-lesion in both contrast phases using the open-source software 3D Slicer. All segmentations were verified by the same resident 4 weeks later. An example of segmentation is shown in Supplementary Fig. S1.

Item9.reason: The study does not include fully automated segmentation.

Item10.reason: The segmentations were performed by a single reader.

Condition3.reason: The study extracts radiomics features using MIRP, which indicates the use of hand-crafted features.

Item11.reason: The study describes a standardization procedure to address variations in contrast medium injection parameters. It also mentions interpolation to an isotropic voxel size and the use of an anti-aliasing filter. The CT images were interpolated to an isotropic voxel size of 1.0 mm3. An anti-aliasing filter was applied, and contours were re-segmented to density values between − 200 and 500 Hounsfield units (HU).

Item12.reason: The study used the publicly available open-source Medical Image Radiomics Processor (MIRP) for feature extraction, which is a standardized software.

Item13.reason: The study mentions the extraction of radiomics features from different segmentations (HCC_art, HCC_ven, HCC_diff, liver_art, liver_ven, liver_diff), resulting in 1146 imaging features per patient. The utilized settings for feature extraction and model building can be found in Supplementary Table S3. The general architecture of the networks was designed after Hosny et al., Starke et al., and Nie et al. and is illustrated in Supplementary Fig. S222,23,24.

Condition4.reason: The study uses tabular data in the form of radiomics features and clinical variables.

Condition5.reason: The study includes end-to-end deep learning models for predicting overall survival.

Item14.reason: The study mentions feature processing steps including filtering. For each model, feature importance was evaluated using a 15-times repeated threefold cross-validation scheme, resulting in 45 internal models in total. In each iteration, multiple feature processing steps were applied: missing value imputation, feature transformation, filtering and clustering.

Item15.reason: The study mentions feature processing steps including clustering. For each model, feature importance was evaluated using a 15-times repeated threefold cross-validation scheme, resulting in 45 internal models in total. In each iteration, multiple feature processing steps were applied: missing value imputation, feature transformation, filtering and clustering.

Item16.reason: The study does not explicitly discuss the appropriateness of dimensionality compared to data size.

Item17.reason: The study does not include a robustness assessment of end-to-end deep learning pipelines.

Item18.reason: The study divides the patients into a development and a validation cohort by stratified randomization, with stratification being performed on the initial therapy concept.

Item19.reason: The study mentions that development and validation cohort were balanced in terms of clinical parameters and baseline demographics.

Item20.reason: Prognostic performance was evaluated by the concordance index (C-index) and the ability to stratify patients into risk groups based on the model predictions. The C-index measures the agreement between the actual OS and the model predictions.

Item21.reason: The study reports 95% confidence intervals (CI) for the C-index values.

Item22.reason: Figure S3 shows the calibration plots of the best performing image-based models from both the conventional radiomics and deep-learning approaches.

Item23.reason: The study does not perform uni-parametric imaging or proof of its inferiority.

Item24.reason: The study does not include a non-radiomic method that is representative of the clinical practice is included in the analysis for comparison purposes.

Item25.reason: The study does not include a comparison with a simple baseline reference model.

Item26.reason: The model is tested on an independent data set that is sampled from the same source as the training and/or validation sets.

Item27.reason: The model is not tested with independent data from other institution(s).

Item28.reason: The datasets used and analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

Item29.reason: The study does not mention sharing all scripts related to automatic segmentation and/or modeling with the public.

Item30.reason: The study does not mention sharing the final model in the form of a raw model file or as a ready-to-use system.
